content:
You have learned how to fetch data seamlessly. But handling state mutations — saving, deleting, and updating — without triggering database race conditions is the true senior engineering challenge. Here is how to replace legacy RxJS spaghetti with the modern mutation pattern.

5 min read
3 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

However, if you’ve done some work implementing the upgrades from a legacy system to Angular, you’ll realize that the nasty truth about network requests is that fetching data is the simple part.
Fetching data is safe by nature. Even if the user clicks “Refresh” five times, the only thing that happens is that you are wasting some bandwidth. De-bouncing that click or cancelling the previously initiated network requests is trivial.
But modifying data (i.e., POST/PUT/DELETE operations) is an entirely different story.
If the user clicks the “Submit Payment” or “Save Profile” buttons twice because he got impatient, you cannot simply cancel the first request since the HTTP packet is long gone, and the database server is already processing the request. If you don’t implement the exact scenario in…
